These have been supplied by the global technical committee to enhance the usage of the Standard or Safety Guideline. 1 Purpose 1.1 The purpose of this Specification is to establish basic physical dimensions for the Tape Frame FOUP which is intended to be used to transport and store 300 mm tape frames, as specified by SEMI G74 and SEMI G87, within IC manufacturing facilities. 2 Scope 2.1 This Standard specifies the external features and dimensions of the Tape Frame FOUP. 2.2 This Standard specifies the internal dimensions for storing the tape frames in the Tape Frame FOUP. 2.3 This Standard specifies the areas

paragraphs.context: specified by SEMI G74 and SEMI G87, within IC manufacturing facilities. 2 Scope 2.1 This Standard specifies the external features and dimensions of the Tape Frame FOUP. 2.2 This Standard specifies the internal dimensions for storing the tape frames in the Tape Frame FOUP. 2.3 This Standard specifies the areas to ensure automated installing and extracting the tape frames by end effectors (EEs). 2.4 This Standard specifies the external dimensions of interfaces for automation tools which are designed for interacting with the Tape Frame FOUP. OTICE: SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address all safety issues associated with their use. It is the responsibility of the users of the Documents to establish appropriate safety and health practices, and determine the applicability of regulatory or other limitations prior to use. 3 Limitations 3.1 The detailed methods and mechanisms inside a 300 mm Tape Frame FOUP door as to how the door is engaged to and disengaged from the shell are not specified by this Specification. 3.2 A fully loaded front-opening unified pod (FOUP) will be too heavy for manual handling during normal production or maintenance activities. It is anticipated that manual handling will only occur when recovering from an abnormal situation. Consequently, there is no provision for manual handles. 4 Referenced Standards and Documents 4.1 SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines SEMI G74 - Specification for Tape Frame for 300 mm Wafers SEMI G87 - Specification for Plastic Tape Frame for 300 mm Wafer OTICE: Unless otherwise indicated, all

These have been supplied by the globaJ technical committee to enhance the usage of the Standard or Safety Guideline. 1 Purpose 1.1 This Standard defines overarching and fundamental cybersecurity requirements as a baseline to secure semiconductor fab equipment by design and support security protection in operation and maintenance. 1.2 This Standard intends to be applied by entities who provide equipment or services to semiconductor fabrication plants such as equipment suppliers and system integrators. 2 Scope 2.1 This Standard addresses required measures for cybersecurity in the design, operation, and maintenance of semiconductor production equipment and automated material handling system. 2.2 This Standard provides fundamental requirements in the following aspects: Operating system (OS) support, Network security, Endpoint protection, and Security monitoring. 2.3 This Standard applies to computing devices offab equipment, which are installed with Microsoft Windows or Linuxu operating system. 2.4 This Standard does not apply to computing devices of programmable logic controllers {PL Cs), supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA), and devices connected to them via sensor-actuator networks. I Ol'erview semr 9.1.1 Vulnerability assessing is a way to determine if vulnerabilities affect the fab equipment, and malware scanning is commonly used to ensure that the fab equipment is protected from malicious software. Thus. pre-shipment vulnerability scan and malware scan of production equipment provide a proven method to prevent malware intrusion. Meanwhile equipment suppliers can provide end users with the ability to install, manage, or maintain endpoint protection mechanisms that can reduce the risk of malware infection in their operations. Moreover, access control with authentication and authorization is required to prevent invasion attacks and unauthorized use. See SEMI E 169 for guidance on authentication and authorization for the information asset. 9.2 Vulnerability Mitigation [El87.00-RQ-00005-00] Equipment suppliers shall perform vulnerability scan prior to equipment shipment and deliver a scanning report, including name and version of scanning tool. scanning scope of coverage. and scanning date, with evidence of no critical severity vulnerability according to common industrial vulnerability scoring standard. This kind of authentication docs not require a memorized secret and usually uses just one highly secure factor to authenticate identity. making ii faster and simpler. Some authentication technology examples arc listed in this Related information. R1-1 Fast Identity Online (FID04D') R 1-1.1 FIDO standard is one example of strong authentication mechanisms, and it is used for authenticating users to online services (by internet or intranet) that do not depend on passwords. That means FIDO can be deployed in factories without internet access or cloud, as a complementary or replacement mechanism for password-based authentication. Rl -1.2 FIDO authentication seeks to use the native security capabilities of the user device to enable strong user authentication and reduce the reliance on passwords. paragraphs.context: uptime. 1.2 This Standard provides a framework for how to mitigate the propagation of malware to manufacturing facilities during capital equipment delivery and support activities. 2 Scope 2.1 This Standard addresses the required measures for information security in manufacturing equipment delivery, installation. and support activities over the course of the manufacturing equipment life cycle within semiconductor manufacturing facilities. 2.2 This Standard defines protection systems and processes that ensure the integrity of manufacturing equipment, specifically defining the requirements for: Controlling access to manufacturing equipment via networks, removable media, etc. Manufacturing equipment installation Manufacturing equipment upgrade and support tasks Manufacturing equipment restoration, such as hard disk drives (HDD) or computer component replacement 2.3 This Standard applies to equipment suppliers, equipment users, and hardware and software component suppliers. This Standard applies to any computing device (e.g .. computers. controllers. programmable logic controllers [PLCs], etc.). 2.4 This Standard applies to any computing devices and systems located in a manufacturing facility used in the production of semiconductors. This includes process, metrology, and material handling equipment. Computing devices provided by the factory, such as manufacturing execution system (MES), material control system MCS, Host Systems etc., are excluded from this Standard. NOTJ E: SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address all safety issues associated with their use. Additional operating system services may be enabled after manufacturing equipment installation with equipment user approval. 9.4 NIST Security Content Automation Protocol (SCAP) 9.4.1 SCAP (pronounced S-CAP) is a suite of open standards for exchanging security automation content. It defines a format and nomenclature by which software flaws and security configuration information is communicated (both to machines and humans). It can be used to find vulnerabilities and provide a way to score these findings so that possible impact can be properly evaluated. Multiple tools from different providers can use the same SCAP Content to perform a specific assessment described by the content. 9.4. 1.1 There are two main pans to SCAP: SCAP Co11te11t -This describes 'secure' configurations agreed to by NIST and industry panners. There are ones defined by NIST, Governmental Authorities. Providers (e.g., Microsoft3, Red Hat", HPu, etc.), and Third Panies (e.g., Centre for Internet Security16 (CIS7), MITRE8, CyberESia.9 etc.). Current versions can be found in the National Checklist Program Repository10 as pan of the National Vulnerability Database (NVD) on the NIST website, or they can be obtained from the SCAP Content creator. SCAP Scanners - These are software applications that compare the target system and applications against the SCAP Content baseline (configuration/patch level, etc.). The Scanner will detect any deviations and produce a repon.

For example, the CVD ID will let you know if a vulnerability in a Microsoft system and a Linux system are the same, regardless of the identifier used in each of those independent issue tracking systems. 9.5.1.3 The Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) is a framework to communicate the characteristics and severity of software vulnerabilities. Each software vulnerability can be represented by a nwneric CVSS score ranging from O to I 0, with 10 being the most severe. The score takes into account the nature of the vulnerability. temporal metrics for characteristics that evolve over the lifetime of the vulnerability, and environmental metrics that account for a particular environment or implementation. It can be used to evaluate vulnerabilities consistently across software suppliers and operating systems. 9.5. 1.4 1ST maintains the ational Vulnerability Database 13 (NVD), a repository of standards-based vulnerability management data. The VD is synchronized with the CVE List and provides additional analysis by assigning a CVSS score to each vulnerability as well as providing a database and a fine-grain search mechanism. The NVD does not add its own identifier----CVE entries are identified by their CVE ID. 'OTE 20: Currently, the NVD process is to add entries within 24 hours of being added to the CVE List, and update with a CVSS score within 2 business days (excluding federal holidays).

paragraphs.context: Scan prior to shipment. R2-2. I. I The equipment supplier identifies which SCAP Content they should execute to satisfy the equipment user's requirements. For example, the equipment user has decided to use SCAP Content from the Defense Information Systems Agency. They list the following SCAP Content: SCAP 1.2 - Microsoft Windows 10 STIG Benchmark, SCAP 1.2 - Microsoft Windows Server 2019. Equipment supplier's manufacturing equipment runs on Windows 10. The SCAP Content they will execute is SCAP 1.2 - Microsoft Windows IO STIG Benchmark. R2-2. I .2 When the system is ready for final installation (all software components are installed and operating system configuration is set), the equipment supplier runs the SCAP Scanner with the appropriate SCAP Content against the target computing devices. (After the scan is run, there should be no additional software installed or operating system configuration changes.) R2-2. I .3 The equipment supplier reviews the SCAP scan results. For each test that failed or had an error, assess their impact ls there an operating system component that should be removed? Is something misconfigured? Attempt to address the issue that caused the test to fail, and repeat the SCAP scan to remove failures and errors that have been addressed. For each remaining failure. document why the test failure is acceptable. For example, if the test fails because the FTP Server is enabled on the operating system, and the computing device requires FTP Server to be running to transfer files off the manufacturing equipment, the test failure is acceptable.

If they have made changes, a fresh SCAP Scan can be executed before the maintenance activity begins. R2-2.2.2 The equipment supplier performs the maintenance activity (e.g., upgrade the equipment control software).
